UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a user's profile in the dating app.

id.description: Unique identifier for the user profile.

location.description: User's current location (e.g., coordinates or address).

Match.title: Match
Match.description: Represents a match between two users.

user1Id.description: Reference to the first user in the match. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Match)

user2Id.description: Reference to the second user in the match. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Match)

ChatMessage.title: ChatMessage
ChatMessage.description: Represents a chat message between two users.

id.description: Unique identifier for the chat message.

senderId.description: Reference to the user who sent the message. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N ChatMessage)

receiverId.description: Reference to the user who received the message. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N ChatMessage)

matchId.description: Reference to the match associated with the message. (Relationship: Match 1:N ChatMessage)

timestamp.description: Timestamp of when the message was sent.

Like.title: Like
Like.description: Represents a user's like or dislike action towards another user.

likerId.description: The ID of the user who is performing the like action.

likeeId.description: The ID of the user who is being liked.

isLike.description: Indicates whether the user liked (true) or disliked (false) the other user.

timestamp.description: Timestamp of when the like/dislike was recorded.

definition.description: Stores user profile data. Path-based ownership ensures only the user can access their own profile.
Top-level collection to store all like/dislike actions. This avoids writing to other users' subcollections, thus preventing permission issues.
Stores match data between two users. Includes denormalized 'users' array for simple participant authorization.
Stores chat messages for a specific match.

firestore.reasoning: The Firestore data structure has been redesigned for improved security and scalability, particularly for the 'like' functionality. Instead of storing 'likes' in subcollections under each user, a top-level `likes` collection is now used. Each document in `/likes` represents a single like action and contains both `likerId` and `likeeId`. This change is critical because it eliminates the need for one user to write to another user's document tree, which was causing 'permission-denied' errors with the previous security rules. This new structure allows for simple, indexed queries to find 'who I liked' (`where('likerId', '==', currentUserId)`) and 'who liked me' (`where('likeeId', '==', currentUserId)`) without complex security rule workarounds. The rest of the structure for users and matches remains the same, as it is already optimized for ownership-based security and efficient data retrieval.
